text: Acute sleep loss (≤6 hours in a 24-hour period) reduces exercise performance by an average of 7.56% across all exercise categories including anaerobic power, speed/power endurance, high-intensity interval exercise, strength, endurance, strength-endurance, and skill.

Sleep deprivation and late restriction (earlier-than-normal waking) consistently impair exercise performance, while early restriction (delayed sleep onset) does not show the same consistent negative effects.
Each additional hour awake prior to exercise decreases performance by approximately 0.4% under sleep deprivation and late-restriction conditions.
Exercise performed in the afternoon/evening (PM) is consistently impaired by sleep loss, while morning (AM) exercise performance is largely unaffected by prior sleep loss.
When sleep loss is unavoidable, scheduling exercise in the morning rather than the afternoon can help maintain performance levels.
If sleep will be shortened, going to bed later (early restriction/delayed onset) is less detrimental to exercise performance than waking earlier than normal (late restriction).
The threshold distinguishing normal sleep from sleep loss in the context of exercise performance impairment is approximately 6 hours per 24-hour period.
